ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Strangozzi alla Norcina. FOODON_00002473 (pasta food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Strangozzi alla Norcina as a composite dish with sausage and truffle cream.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Strangozzi alla Norcina or a sufficiently close pasta dish composite. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: truffle shaving or truffle paste incorporation

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of Strangozzi alla Norcina is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of fresh egg pasta topped with pork sausage in a truffle-infused cream sauce finished with grated Parmesan.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with meat sauce, homemade-style (SR Legacy) and component ingredient data for cream, pork sausage, and Parmesan cheese
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or primary regional entry exists for Strangozzi alla Norcina. Values are estimated by weighted component assembly: fresh egg pasta (~50% by weight), Italian pork sausage (~25%), heavy cream (~12%), Parmesan cheese (~8%), olive oil and aromatics (~5%). USDA SR Legacy entries used: cooked fresh egg pasta (FDC ID 168930), Italian pork sausage cooked (FDC ID 174608), heavy whipping cream (FDC ID 170859), Parmesan cheese grated (FDC ID 171251). Truffle contributes negligible macronutrients at typical usage levels. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for a restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fresh Pasta and Egg-Based Pasta Products — University of Sydney GI Database; reference entry: fresh pasta, egg-based, boiled (GI ~40–50)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Strangozzi alla Norcina as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fresh egg-based pasta (boiled), which reports GI values in the range 40–50 (reference food: glucose = 100). The cream and sausage components lower the effective GI of the dish relative to plain pasta by slowing gastric emptying. GI of 45 is a conservative mid-range estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 76.3) / 100 ≈ 34.3, rounded to 34.2. Serving carbohydrate: 21.8 g per 100g × 350g serving = 76.3g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cured pork sausage, Parmesan cheese, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100g (approximately 1470 mg per restaurant serving), individuals with hypertension should consume this dish occasionally and request reduced-salt preparation where possible, as a single serving may contribute over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from fresh egg pasta carbohydrates, partially offset by fat and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 45 (low) but a high glycemic load per restaurant serving (~34 g GL), individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 150–200g and pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from heavy cream, Parmesan cheese, and pork sausage
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: At approximately 6.8g saturated fat per 100g (approximately 23.8g per restaurant serving), this dish contributes substantially to the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g for adults; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence and prefer smaller portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Strangozzi alla Norcina is a traditional Italian pasta dish originating from Umbria, central Italy, specifically associated with the town of Norcia, which is renowned for its high-quality pork products and black truffles. Strangozzi are thick, hand-rolled fresh egg pasta noodles with a chewy, rustic texture similar to thick spaghetti or pici, made without eggs in some regional variations but commonly with egg in restaurant preparations. The sauce — alla Norcina — is a rich, aromatic cream sauce built from crumbled or sliced Italian pork sausage (salsiccia di Norcia), heavy cream, garlic, and grated Parmesan cheese, elevated with the earthy, pungent aroma of black Umbrian truffle (Tuber melanosporum) or truffle paste. The dish is finished with freshly ground black pepper and additional Parmesan. Nutritionally, Strangozzi alla Norcina is a calorie-dense, protein-moderate, and fat-rich dish, with approximately 245 kcal per 100g, 10.2g protein, 13.5g fat (of which 6.8g is saturated), and 21.8g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated due to the cured sausage and Parmesan, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low (approximately 45) due to the fresh pasta matrix and the fat-protein buffering effect of the cream and sausage, but the glycemic load per restaurant serving is high due to portion size. Clinically, this dish is best consumed occasionally by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, with attention to portion control. It is a signature dish of Umbrian cuisine and appears on Italian restaurant menus globally, including in Singapore at establishments such as Ricciotti.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Strangozzi alla Norcina exists in any primary regional or USDA database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly from USDA SR Legacy entries for fresh egg pasta, Italian pork sausage, heavy cream, and Parmesan cheese. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fresh egg-based pasta. Confidence is medium because the component-level sources are approved and defensible, but the composite assembly introduces estimation uncertainty, particularly for the cream and sausage proportions which vary by restaurant.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy data; no direct database entry exists for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 2. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (fresh egg pasta, boiled); the cream and protein components are expected to lower the effective GI relative to plain pasta. 3. The restaurant source (Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, Singapore) serves this as a premium pasta dish at SGD 30.52; portion size estimated at 350g based on typical Italian restaurant pasta serving norms. 4. Truffle content is variable and may be truffle paste, truffle oil, or fresh truffle shavings depending on preparation; macronutrient contribution is negligible at typical usage levels. 5. This is an Italian dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. No Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names are required or provided. 6. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and all micronutrients except vitamin D are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are reported as best-estimate values. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for the component ingredients used.
